subject: PhD Algebra
instructions: Answer seven problems. Write your answers clearly in complete English sentences. You may quote results (within reason) as long as you state them clearly.

problems.text: Let \(K\) be the splitting field of \(x^7-2\) over \(\mathbb{Q}\). What is \([K:\mathbb{Q}]\)? Justify your answer.

Give an example of a Galois extension of \(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)\) with degree \(5\) (where \(\zeta_5\) is a primitive 5th root of unity). Give an example of a Galois extension of \(\mathbb{Q}\) with degree \(5\). Justify your answers.
Let \(K\) be a field and \(G\) a finite subgroup of \(\operatorname{Aut}(K)\).

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: What does the “theorem of the fixed field” say about \([K:K^G]\)?

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Use the theorem of the fixed field to show that if \(K/F\) is a finite extension of fields then \(\#\operatorname{Aut}(K/F)\leq [K:F]\) with equality if and only if \(F\) is the fixed field of \(\operatorname{Aut}(K/F)\).

subparts.label: (iii)
subparts.text: Use the theorem of the fixed field to show that if \(F=K^G\) then \(K/F\) is a Galois extension with Galois group \(G\).

problems.text: Let \(\mathrm{Groups}\) denote the category of groups and \(\mathrm{Sets}\) denote the category of sets.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: Use free groups to construct a left adjoint to the forgetful functor \(U:\mathrm{Groups}\to\mathrm{Sets}\). (You can skip checking “naturality” i.e. compatibility with morphisms.)

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text:
Show that the functor \(F:\mathrm{Groups}\to\mathrm{Sets}\) defined by
\[
F(G)=\{(x,y)\in G\times G:xyx^{-1}y^{-1}=1\}
\]
is a representable functor. (Again, you can skip checking “naturality”.)

problems.text: Let \(k\) be a field. For a \(k\)-vector space \(V\), recall that the dual vector space \(V^*\) is defined to be the \(k\)-vector space of linear functionals on \(V\), i.e. linear transformations from \(V\) to \(k\).

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: Construct a natural linear transformation \(\psi:V^*\otimes_k W^*\to(V\otimes_k W)^*\).

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Show that \(\psi\) is an isomorphism if \(V\) and \(W\) are finite dimensional.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: State at least two equivalent definitions of a projective \(R\)-module. (You don’t need to show they are equivalent, but you should include any definition you want to use in the next part.)

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Let \(M\) and \(N\) be \(R\)-modules with \(M\subset N\). If \(M\) is projective and \(N/M\) is projective, prove that \(N\) is projective.

problems.text: Are the functors \(F(A)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}}(A,\mathbb{Q}/\mathbb{Z})\) and \(G(A)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb{Z}}(A,\mathbb{Z})\) from the category of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules to the category of \(\mathbb{Z}\)-modules exact? Justify your answer.

Prove that the intersection of all prime ideals in a commutative ring is the nilradical. (Suggestion: for one direction, given a non-nilpotent element \(a\) consider the set of all ideals not containing any power of \(a\).)
Let \(\mathbb{R}\) and \(\mathbb{C}\) denote the real and complex numbers as usual.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: Give an example of two different maximal ideals of \(\mathbb{R}[x]\) which have the same zero set in the affine line \(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{R}}\).

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Give an example of two ideals of \(\mathbb{C}[x]\) which have the same zero set in \(\mathbb{A}^1_{\mathbb{C}}\).

problems.text: Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and \(D\) a multiplicatively closed subset of \(R\) containing \(1\). State the universal property for the localization \(D^{-1}R\), show that any two rings satisfying the universal property are isomorphic, and construct a ring satisfying the universal property. (Checking the construction is well-defined will involve a variety of verifications which you can skip. But do check it satisfies the universal property.)
Give an example of the following, and briefly justify your answers:

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: A discrete valuation ring whose field of fractions is not of characteristic zero.

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: A Dedekind domain that is not a unique factorization domain.
